coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of broad spectrum-driven photocatalysts waste biomass-derived carbon quantum dots/g-C3N4 with superior energy bands for PPCPs restoration
coredata.dc:creator: Huang, Fei
Tan, Dongyu
Li, Danqin
Guo, Shenjia
Yan, Yi
Zhang, Wu
coredata.dc:description: Herein, for the first time, waste pine needles were applied to prepare carbon quantum dots (CQDs). The CQDs were successfully loaded onto graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) afterward. The obtained CQDs/g-C3N4 exhibits stronger optical absorption and broad-spectrum response. Using CQDs/g-C3N4 as a catalyst, the degradation ratio towards tetracycline hydrochloride (TC) can be achieved to 93% within 10 min, which is 12.56 times that of pure g-C3N4. The excellent photocatalytic activity can be attributed to the up-conversion luminescence properties of CQDs, which possess an unexpectedly narrow band gap (1.58 eV), and the ability to promote the separation and transfer of photocarriers generated by O2 •-. The degradation mechanism was investigated by radical trapping experiments, EPR, LC-MS, and DFT calculations. Subsequently, the TC degradation pathways and the toxicity of the intermediates were analyzed. In addition, catalyst dosage, TC concentration, pH, coexisting ions, and a natural water matrix were investigated, all of which showed excellent photocatalytic activity and good cycling stability. Thus, CQDs/g-C3N4 composite provides a strategy for broad-spectrum sunlight utilization as well as a promising approach for the environmentally compatible and cost-effective remediation of wastewater containing pharmaceuticals and personal care products (PPCPs).

row.entry: Degradation rate and concentration of TC

head.ce:title: Synthesis of broad spectrum-driven photocatalysts waste biomass-derived carbon quantum dots/g-C3N4 with superior energy bands for PPCPs restoration

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Marine and Environment Science, Tianjin Key Laboratory of Marine Resources and Chemistry, Tianjin Marine Environmental Protection and Restoration Technology Engineering Center, Tianjin University of Science and Technology, Tianjin 300457, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Marine and Environment Science, Tianjin Key Laboratory of Marine Resources and Chemistry, Tianjin Marine Environmental Protection and Restoration Technology Engineering Center, Tianjin University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Marine and Environment Science, Tianjin Key Laboratory of Marine Resources and Chemistry, Tianjin Marine Environmental Protection and Restoration Technology Engineering Center, Tianjin University of Science and Technology, Tianjin 300457, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, for the first time, waste pine needles were applied to prepare carbon quantum dots (CQDs). The CQDs were successfully loaded onto graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) afterward. The obtained CQDs/g-C3N4 exhibits stronger optical absorption and broad-spectrum response. Using CQDs/g-C3N4 as a catalyst, the degradation ratio towards tetracycline hydrochloride (TC) can be achieved to 93% within 10 min, which is 12.56 times that of pure g-C3N4. The excellent photocatalytic activity can be attributed to the up-conversion luminescence properties of CQDs, which possess an unexpectedly narrow band gap (1.58 eV), and the ability to promote the separation and transfer of photocarriers generated by O2 •-. The degradation mechanism was investigated by radical trapping experiments, EPR, LC-MS, and DFT calculations. Subsequently, the TC degradation pathways and the toxicity of the intermediates were analyzed. In addition, catalyst dosage, TC concentration, pH, coexisting ions, and a natural water matrix were investigated, all of which showed excellent photocatalytic activity and good cycling stability. Thus, CQDs/g-C3N4 composite provides a strategy for broad-spectrum sunlight utilization as well as a promising approach for the environmentally compatible and cost-effective remediation of wastewater containing pharmaceuticals and personal care products (PPCPs).

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Pharmaceuticals and personal care products (PPCPs) are widely consumed in everyday life and continuously discharged with domestic and industrial wastewater[1]. The increasing accumulation of PPCPs poses threats to the ecological environment and human health[2]. Traditional wastewater treatment technologies are not effective in removing PPCPs owing to low biodegradability, strong stability, and ecotoxicity[3]. Therefore, it has become a global challenge to exploit environmentally compatible strategies for eliminating PPCPs efficiently.

Photocatalysis is known as one of the most promising technologies for alleviating the energy crisis and eliminating various environmental contaminants[4,5]. A great deal of semiconductor materials has been currently certified to be applicable for removing pollutants, among which g-C3N4 presents as a promising photocatalyst candidate due to its non-toxicity, suitable band gap (2.7 eV), high stability, and unique optical properties. It has been widely used in eliminating pollutants[6,7], hydrogen evolution[8,9] and reducing CO2 emission[10,11]. However, the further applications of g-C3N4 were restricted due to its inherent defects, such as low specific surface area, easy recombination of photogenerated carriers, poor visible light absorption, and fairly low quantum efficiency. Various strategies have been developed to optimize the photocatalytic activity of g-C3N4, including elemental doping[12–14], micro-anodization[15], coupling with other semiconductors or metals[16,17], and crystal structure tuning[18]. However, the above-modified g-C3N4 were prepared by complex and expensive methods, which were sometimes accompanied by potential contamination. It is essential to develop a low-cost, simple preparation route for photocatalyst with high activity.

Notably, carbon-modification is an effective method for improving the catalytic performance of g-C3N4 [19,20]. As a new type of carbon nanomaterial, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) exhibit unique and excellent physicochemical and optoelectronic properties, such as low toxicity, strong chemical inertness, high water solubility, up-conversion photoluminescence, exceptional electron conversion ability, and photo-bleaching resistance[21–24]. Besides, they are low-cost, environmentally friendly, and widely available, which gradually piqued great researchers’ interest in recent years. Riaz et al.[25] demonstrated that the introduction of CQDs into g-C3N4 could promote photoelectron transport and increase visible light absorption. Recently, Li et al.[26] discovered that the different attachment positions of CQDs on the catalyst resulted in different electron transfer paths. Based on the above discussion, CQDs/g-C3N4 may be an ideal metal-free composite photocatalyst for abating PPCPs pollution.

Recently, Jin et al. published the preparation of CQDs/g-C3N4 nanocomposites with excellent photocatalytic activity toward PPCPs[27]. However, the process was complicated and time-consuming, including (1) synthesizing CQDs by pyrolysis of citric acid and urea, (2) obtaining ultrathin g-C3N4 by calcination of melamine and NH4Cl, and (3) preparing CQDs/g-C3N4 nanocomposites by grinding NaBH4, ultrathin g-C3N4, CQDs mixture, and calcination again. Ding et al. found that CQDs/g-C3N4 hollow spheres represented strong photo-absorption capability, large specific surface area and carrier migration, which exhibited excellent photocatalytic activity for hydrogen evolution[28]. Similarly, the preparation procedure was inconvenient, too. First, the preparation of SiO2/mSiO2 hard templates took at least one day. Secondly, the hard template was mixed with monocyanine and glucose, dried, and calcined. Finally, the template was removed by NH4HF2.

Herein, CQDs/g-C3N4 composites were obtained through calcining the mixture of CQDs and urea, in which CQDs were synthesized by a simple hydrothermal process from waste pine needles for the first time. The hydrophilicity of CQDs facilitated its anchoring on various semiconductor photocatalysts in an aqueous solution. CQDs/g-C3N4 showed an unexpected energy band gap (1.58 eV) and broad-spectrum response. The morphologies, compositions and optical properties were studied through a series of characterizations, such as scanning electron microscope (SEM), high resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM), X-ray diffraction (XRD), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), UV–visible absorption, photoluminescence (PL), et al. And the photocatalytic activities of obtained composites were investigated with adjusting photocatalyst dosages, pH, initial TC concentrations and co-existing ions. The degradation mechanism of TC was investigated by reactive species trapping experiments, electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR), liquid chromatograph mass spectrometer (LC-MS), and density functional theory (DFT) calculations. Subsequently, the degradation pathways of TC and the toxicity of intermediates were analyzed, and a potential photocatalytic mechanism for CQDs/g-C3N4 was proposed. Eventually, the practical feasibility of CQDs/g-C3N4 for the degradation of TC in different aqueous mediums was investigated. Compared to the reported work[27,28], the preparation method in this study is facile and environmentally friendly. It is noteworthy that the starting material (pine needle) for synthesizing CQDs is readily available as biomass waste. This study provides a valuable avenue to develop novel sunlight-driven nonmetallic photocatalysts, which could be economical, efficient, and stable.

Urea (CH4N2O, 99%), ascorbic acid (AA), sodium oxalate (Na2C2O4), isopropanol (IPA), and L-Histidine (L-His) were obtained from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. Tetracycline hydrochloride (C22H24N2O8HCl) was obtained from Beijing Solar Technology Co., Ltd. NaCl, NaNO3, Na2SO4, NaHCO3, MgSO4, and Cu(NO3)2 were purchased from Tianjin Jinke Fine Chemical Research Institute. All above chemicals were used without further purification. Waste pine needles were gathered from Chengde City, Hebei Province. The experimental water was deionized water (DW).

Firstly, the waste pine needles were dried, ground, and sieved into 300 µm powder. 4 g of the above powder and 80 mL of deionized water were mixed and transferred into a 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave. After being heated at 220 °C for 10 h, the autoclave was cooled down to room temperature. The light-yellow liquid was gathered and filtered by a membrane with 0.8 µm, and the solution containing CQDs were obtained.

Above solution (0–12 mL) was transferred into an alumina crucible containing 10 g of urea. The mixture was dried at 50 ℃ under continuous stirring, then calcined at 550 °C for 4 h. The obtained brown sample was milled into powder with an agate mortar after cooling down to room temperature. The resulting products were labeled “Hx”, where “x” represented the volume (mL) of the CQDs solution. It was worth noting that the pure g-C3N4 prepared without carbon dots was labeled as “H0”.

The photocatalytic activity was evaluated in a quartz tube with a 350 W xenon lamp as the visible light source. The degradation efficiency was investigated by mixing 10 mg CQDs/g-C3N4 with 100 mL of TC solution (5 mg/L). The suspension was stirred in the dark for 30 min to achieve adsorption-desorption equilibrium before irradiation. During irradiation, 3 mL solution was collected in a given interval and filtered with 22 µm membrane. Finally, the TC concentration was determined using a UV–vis spectrophotometer at 375 nm.

As shown in Fig. 1A, CQDs/g-C3N4 was prepared through a simple thermal polycondensation process. CQDs were first synthesized using waste pine needles as starting material with a hydrothermal method. Fig. 1D showed that CQDs had an average size of about 2.73 nm (Nano Measurer 1.2 software). The distinct lattice stripes with a d‐spacing-spacing of 0.21 nm could be observed in the HRTEM image (inset of Fig. 1D), which corresponded to the (101) lattice plane of graphitic carbon. H0 was composed of highly condensed two-dimensional sheets with smooth surfaces and chiffon-like wrinkles and ripples stacking along the c-axis (SEM, Fig. 1B, and TEM, Fig. 1E)[29]. The SEM (Fig. 1C) and TEM (Fig. 1F) images of H6 showed that the average diameter of CQDs on g-C3N4 was 2.71 nm. HRTEM images also clearly showed lattice stripes with a spacing of 0.21 nm for CQDs (Fig. 1F inset), which corresponded well with the (101) spacing of graphitic carbon, indicating that CQDs have been successfully anchored to g-C3N4. In addition, the EDS mapping (Fig. 1G) showed that carbon (C), nitrogen (N), and oxygen (O) elements were uniformly distributed throughout the sample, further indicating the successful doping of CQDs into g-C3N4.

The structures of pure g-C3N4 and hybrid catalysts (H0, H4, H6, H8, H12) were investigated by XRD. As shown in Fig. 2A, all samples exhibited similar peaks, indicating successful CQDs deposition onto g-C3N4, which were in agreement with the TEM observations. For g-C3N4, a strong diffraction peak at 27.08° and a weak one at 13.52° were observed, which could be ascribed to the interlayer stacking of the conjugated aromatic and the in-plane stacking of the tri-s-triazine unit, respectively[30]. It was noteworthy that the intensity of both peaks decreased with the increase of CQDs, which could be attributed to the fact that the CQDs prevented polycondensation of urea and the damage caused by the interlayer stacking after heat treatment[31,32]. In addition, a shift of the peak of the (002) plane from 27.14° in H0 to 26.74° in H12 was observed (Fig. 2A inset). According to Bragg's law (
2 d ∙ sin
θ
= n ∙ λ
, where d is the layer spacing, θ is the diffraction angle in the crystal plane, and λ is the X-ray wavelength), the decrease of diffraction angle corresponded to an increase in the interlayer distance of the conjugated aromatic structure from 0.328 nm in H0 to 0.333 nm in H6. It could be due to the introduction of oxygen in CQDs or the breakage of the C-N bond[33]. Owing to the intense interfacial interaction between CQDs and g-C3N4, as well as the low CQDs content, the distortion of the g-C3N4 crystal structure was negligible.

In FTIR spectra (Fig. 2B), the peak at 809.16 cm−1 was responsible for the bending of the s-triazine unit, and some vibration bands at 1000–1700 cm−1 could be ascribed to CN stretching vibrations, all of which could confirm the formation of the graphitic carbon nitride structure. After the modification of CQDs, the peaks at 1238 cm−1 corresponded to the vibrations of the C-O-C group and exhibited a slight enhancement of C-O stretching, indicating the binding of oxygen atoms[34,35]. The N-H or O-H stretching vibrations were represented by the peak at 3000–3500 cm−1 [36]. It was shown that H0 and serials of Hx composites exhibited almost the same FTIR spectra. These results demonstrated that the chemical structure of g-C3N4 was well remained after CQDs modification.

The XPS spectrum (Fig. 2 C) showed that all photocatalysts were composed of C, N, and a small amount of O, which might come from the substrate urea. The C/N ratio increased from 1.30 (H0) to 1.48 (H6) along with the introduction of CQDs (Table S1). Three peaks in the high-resolution C 1 s spectra (Fig. 2D) corresponded to graphitic carbon (C-C), hydroxyl carbon (C-OH), and sp2 hybridized carbon (N = C-N), respectively[37]. It should be noted that the relative quantities of C-C and C-OH of H6 were slightly higher than those of H0, which demonstrated the successful doping of CQDs (Table S2). In the N 1 s spectrum (Fig. 2E), it was observed that photocatalysts showed peaks at 398.94 eV, 400.82 eV, and 404.74 eV, which could be responsible for the sp2 hybridized nitrogen (CN-C), amino (C-NH), and N atoms of the graphitic aromatic triazine ring[38]. While the peaks of the O 1 s spectrum (Fig. 2 F) at 531.76 eV and 532.68 eV represented C-OH and C-O-C, respectively[39]. In addition, the C-OH proportion increased from 64.65% to 69.67% due to the resident hydroxyl groups of CQDs (Table S3). The XPS results demonstrated that the incorporation of CQDs into the polymer matrix has not significantly changed the chemical structure of the obtained carbon nitride, which coincided with the FT-IR results.

The UV–vis absorption spectrum ( Fig. 3A) showed that CQDs solution exhibited a broad band from 190 nm to 800 nm, with the dominant peak at 240 nm originating from the absorptions of aromatic rings. A bright blue luminescence was observed under 365 nm UV irradiation (Fig. 3A inset), which indicated that CQDs exhibited down-conversion fluorescence properties. In Fig. 3B, it can be observed that CQDs would emit shorter wavelengths light (311–553 nm) when they were excited at longer wavelengths (550–900 nm), which indicated that CQDs possessed significant up-conversion luminescence properties[40]. The excellent luminescence of CQDs would facilitate the optical absorption of g-C3N4.

In UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (DRS), compared with g-C3N4, the absorption bands of composites exhibited a significant red shift (Fig. 3C), in other words, which indicated that CQDs could extend the visible absorption edges from 440 nm to 543 nm. Notably, after the introduction of CQDs, it was observed that strong absorption exhibited from UV to NIR region (200–800 nm), indicating that CQDs could effectively extend the spectral response range of g-C3N4. The band gap energies (Eg) of H0, H4, H6, H8, and H12 were determined to be 2.68 eV, 2.15 eV, 1.58 eV, 1.82 eV, and 1.66 eV, respectively, according to the Tauc method[41] (Fig. 3D). The narrower band gap would be benefit for the solar utilization and the generation of additional photoinduced carriers, which further confirmed that the introduction of CQDs significantly improved the efficiency of solar absorption.

The energy band structure was determined by Mott-Schottky (M-S) and valence band X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (VB-XPS) to further explore the carrier separation mechanism. As shown in Fig. 3E, the flat-band potentials of H0 and H6 were ascertained to be − 0.82 eV and − 0.41 eV (vs. Hg/Hg2Cl2 electrodes), corresponding to − 0.60 eV and − 0.19 eV (vs. NHE), respectively. The fitted linear plot had a positive slope, indicating that the serial photocatalysts were typical n-type semiconductors whose flat-band potential approximately equaled the Fermi energy level (Ef)[42]. Thus, the Ef was − 0.60 eV for H0 and − 0.19 eV for H6 (vs. NHE). Fig. 3 F showed that the energy gaps between the Ef and the valence band (VB) were 2.24 eV and 1.46 eV for H0 and H6, respectively. Accordingly, the valence bands (VB) of H0 and H6 could be calculated as 1.64 and 1.27 eV, respectively. Meanwhile, the CB positions of − 1.04 eV and − 0.31 eV (VB=CB+Eg) were calculated for H0 and H6, respectively, based on the Eg results, which are schematically shown in Fig. 4A. It could be observed that the narrower band gap of H6 is accompanied by a positive shift in CB and VB, which was due to the electron accumulation effect of CQDs. In addition, the upward transfer of the CB favored the generation of photoelectrons, which was attributed to the high reduction ability of CB photoelectrons[43]. Notably, since CQDs have a low Fermi energy level (Ef) (0 eV vs. NHE)[44], there was a Schottky barrier when electrons transferred from g-C3N4 to CQDs[45], which shortened the transfer path of electrons, resulting in more effective separation of electron-hole pairs. Therefore, the electronic structure of H6 was anticipated to favor the generation of photoinduced carriers, hence improving the photocatalytic performance.

It was confirmed that higher PL intensity implied a higher recombination rate[46]. Compared to g-C3N4, the PL intensity of composites showed a significant decrease, indicating that the introduction of CQDs could suppress the recombination of electron-hole pairs due to the efficient charge transfer (Fig. 4B). In Fig. S1, the PL spectra showed an ascent after an initial decline with the increase of CQDs. H6 had the lowest emission intensity, namely the highest separation efficiency of the electron-hole pair. In addition, transient photocurrent spectra and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) were used to evaluate the charge transfer efficiency. As shown in Fig. 4 C, a rapid photocurrent response of the samples was observed upon irradiation. The hybrid materials exhibited higher photocurrent intensities contrast to g-C3N4. In a Nyquist plot based on EIS (Fig. 4D), the arc radius of H6 was significantly smaller than that of H0, which indicated that H6 had a lower electron transfer resistance. The loading of CQDs remarkably improved the separation efficiency and transport characteristics of photocarriers.

CQDs/g-C3N4 exhibited excellent photocatalytic activity on the degradation of various PPCPs under visible light irradiation (Fig. S2). Specifically, the degradation rates of tetracycline hydrochloride, oxytetracycline, chlortetracycline, ciprofloxacin, and sulfamethoxazole were 90.54%, 83.34%, 67.16%, 61.49%, and 24.69%, respectively. It indicated that CQDs/g-C3N4 displayed good versatility for the photocatalytic degradation of various PPCPs.

TC was chosen as the model pollutant to investigate the photocatalytic performance of the CQDs/g-C3N4 composite. As shown in Fig. S3, the adsorption equilibrium could be attained within 30 min in the dark. Fig. 5A showed the photocatalytic degradation of TC by serial CQDs/g-C3N4 composites. Notably, the self-photodegradation of TC was negligible after 150 min due to its high stability under visible light. The removal rate of TC was only 40% after 150 min when H0 was used as a photocatalyst. By contrast, those of H4, H6, H8, and H12 were significantly increased to 83.20%, 90.54%, 77.23%, and 74.22%, respectively, which indicated that CQDs were essential for potentiating the photocatalytic performance. In addition, H6 was used as a model photocatalyst in the following experiments since it exhibited the highest removal efficiency.

Moreover, the kinetics of photocatalytic degradation were investigated in Fig. S4, in which all photocatalysts could be described by the second-order kinetic model. Fig. 5B showed that the degradation rate of H6 was 0.01186 L•mol−1•s−1, which is 12.56 times higher than that of H0. The excellent photocatalytic activity could be attributed to the remarkable optical properties and charge transfer ability of CQDs. However, the degradation efficiency of TC decreased with a further increase of CQDs loading, which might be ascribed to the light-shielding effect of CQDs. Furthermore, excess CQDs could also act as a recombination center, thus reducing the photoresponse of g-C3N4.

Subsequently, the influence of photocatalyst dosage within the ranges of 0.05–0.7 g/L was evaluated (Fig. 5 C). The elimination rate of TC considerably increased with the photocatalyst concentration from 0.05 to 0.5 g/L under a 150 min exposure to visible light. While a further increase in photocatalyst dosing did not give rise to a significant enhancement of the TC degradation rate. This could be due to granule aggregation and light scattering brought on by excess photocatalysts. Therefrom 0.5 g/L was chosen as the optimum dosage considering the balance between low cost and TC removal rate.

The initial PPCPs concentration is variable in practical wastewater. Therefore, degradation of TC at different initial concentrations were carried out to evaluate the prevalence of CQDs/g-C3N4. As shown in Fig. 5D, 1 mg/L of TC could be decomposed utterly within 45 min. Even the initial TC concentration reached 20 mg/L, the degradation rate still achieved 54.05% within 150 min. Hence it suggested that the CQDs/g-C3N4 composites could be universally applied to the disposal of wastewater pollutants with different concentrations.

As the general background electrolytes in water, Cl-, NO3 -, SO4 2-, HCO3 -, Mg2+ and Cu2+ often coexist in the effluent. Therefore, the interference of natural water components were investigated. As shown in Fig. 5E, the influence of Cl-, NO3 -, and SO4 2- on the photocatalytic degradation of TC were negligible. Notably, it showed a moderately inhibition of degradation in the presence of HCO3 -, which might be associated the quenching of •OH by HCO3 - with a secondary rate constant of 8.5 × 106 L•mol−1•s−1 to generating the less reactive •CO3 - species[27]. The above results also indicated that •OH was not the primary radical for TC degradation. The mild decrease by Mg2+ could be attributed to the fact that Mg2+ could be adsorbed on the catalyst surface and reduce the active sites. Furthermore, significant inhibition by Cu2+ may be due to its higher secondary reaction rate constant with O2 •-, which allowed the reaction of O2 •- with Cu2+ to proceed prior to the degradation of TC[47]. Nevertheless, the results still illustrate the great potential of H6 for tolerance and applicability in practical water matrices.

The pH value is also an overwhelming factor affecting the performance of photocatalysts in practical water treatment. The degradation rate of TC at different pH values was investigated (Fig. 5 F), and the reaction rate constants were shown in inset. The best photocatalytic activity (Kobs=0.00967 L•mol−1•s−1) was achieved when initial pH value was 6.7, since the neutral conditions favored the oxidation of OH- or H2O by h+ to generate •OH, which in turn improved the removal of TC molecules by •OH[48]. In addition, TC could transit to less stable photoexcited states absorbing photons when the solution pH value was close to the equipotential point of TC, thus the decomposition rate was accelerated[49]. It should be noted that neither acidic nor alkaline conditions were favorable for degradation, especially strong bases (pH=11), which significantly lowered TC removal efficiency. It could be owing to the zero-point charge (pHPZC) of H6 and the charge properties of TC. Fig. S5 showed the zeta potential of H6 at different charges, and the calculated pHPZC of H6 was 5.84, indicating that the surface of H6 was positive charge at pH< 5.84 and negative charge at pH> 5.84. Accordingly, TC existed in three forms at different pH levels: protonated (TCH3 +) at pH< 3.3, neutral (TCH2 0) at pH 3.3–7.7, monoanionic (TC-) at pH> 7.7[50]. When pH< 3.3, TCH3 + was repelled from the positively charged surface of H6. While pH> 7.7, TC exists as a single anion. The negative charge raised with the increase of pH, which inhibited ROS. Based on the above discussion, it could be inferred that there would be strong electrostatic repulsions between TC and H6 at pH= 3.0, 9.0, and 11.0, resulting in lower adsorption capacities for TC molecules on the catalyst, thus reduced the photocatalytic activity of H6. The CQD/g-C3N4 composites maintained high photocatalytic activity over a wide pH (3−11) range, indicating that they were beneficial for practical applications.

The sustainability of the catalyst is a critical characteristic in practical applications. Herein, the stability and reusability of H6 were evaluated by recycling tests. The degradation rate of TC remained around 80% after five cycles of reaction ( Fig. 6 A), indicating that H6 had high stability and could be used for repeated treatment of TC solutions. In addition, XRD and FTIR (Fig. 6B-C) were adopted to further investigate the physicochemical stability of H6, and the results showed no significant changes in the crystalline and chemical structures of H6. It could be concluded that H6 was sustainable and stable in the practical treatment of wastewater.

So as to investigate the photodegradation mechanism, L-His, AA, Na2C2O4, and IPA were used as quenchers of singlet oxygen (1O2), superoxide radicals (O2 •-), photogenerated vacancies (h+), and hydroxyl radicals (•OH), respectively. Fig. 7 A showed that the degradation efficiency of TC decreased rapidly with the introduction of AA and L-His, indicating that O2 •- and 1O2 were the primary active species in the degradation of TC. Notably, the most significant decrease in TC removal (from 90.54% to 16.32%) was observed with the addition of AA, so it could be inferred that O2 •- was the predominant reactive radical. Then, introducing Na2C2O4 resulted in a low removal rate of 67.93%, confirming that h+ played a minor role in TC photodegradation. It was noteworthy that the photocatalytic performance of the combination of L-His and Na2C2O4 (34.59%) was lower than that of L-His alone (39.12%), indicating that 1O2 was produced by the reaction of O2 •- with h+ [51]. Furthermore, the removal rate was reduced by 7.27% in the presence of IPA, indicating that •OH was not the dominant active species in this reaction.

To further confirm the presence of active substances in the above photocatalysis process, ESR analysis was performed with DMPO as a spin trap. As depicted in Fig. 7B-C, there was hardly a ESR signal being demonstrated in the dark. The characteristic four-line ESR signals of DMPO-O2 •- and DMPO-•OH were found under xenon light illumination, indicating that both H0 and H6 produced O2 •- and •OH. Notably, the signals of H6 on DMPO-O2 •- and DMPO-•OH were substantially stronger than those of H0, which indicated that the introduction of CQDs promoted the generation of additional O2 •- and •OH, and consequently enhanced the photocatalytic activity. As expected, the ESR signal of DMPO-O2 •- was significantly greater than that of DMPO-•OH with visible light illumination. Since the redox potential at the VB was more minus than that of HO- or H2O[52], OH- and H2O could not be oxidized to •OH by h+ in H6. Only the partial oxidation of H+ by O2 •- produced the •OH radicals[37]. •OH and h+ played a supporting function in the photocatalysis process, whereas O2 •- and 1O2 was the main active species, which were consistent with the above trapping experiments.

Fig. 8 depicted a possible photocatalytic mechanism of CQDs/g-C3N4 based on the previous results. When exposed to sunlight, g-C3N4 absorbed UV and visible light with wavelengths shorter than 440 nm and generated electron-hole pairs. Following the introduction of CQDs, visible and near-infrared light would be converted to short-wavelength light that g-C3N4 could absorb, and g-C3N4 would be irradiated to generate electrons and holes. In addition, the band gap that has been narrowed from 2.68 eV to 1.58 eV could enhance the utilization of sunlight, resulting in additional photoinduced carriers.

Micro-regional heterostructures of CQDs and g-C3N4, from the other hand, could alter the electron dispersion, promote the transfer of photogenerated charges among the tris-triazine ring units, and effectively hinder photogenerated carrier recombination[53]. Also, the electron acceptor effect and excellent charge transfer properties of CQDs resulted in efficient separation of electron-hole pairs so that electrons can effectively migrate to the photocatalyst surface (Eq. 1). These surface electrons combined with oxygen to generate O2 •- (Eq. 2)[54] and followed by the reaction with h+ to produce 1O2 (Eq. 3). Notably, since the redox potential at the VB was more minus than that of HO- or H2O, h+ in H6 could not oxidize OH- and H2O to •OH. Only the partial oxidation of H+ by O2 •- produced the •OH radicals (Eq. 4). Finally, the TC adsorbed on the photocatalyst surface was attacked by various RSs (O2 •-, 1O2, h+, and •OH), resulting in its decomposition and mineralization (Eq. 5)[55].
(1) CQDs/g-C3N4 + hυ → e- + h+
(2) O2 + e- → O2 •-
(3) O2 •- + h+ → 1O2
(4) O2 •- + H+ → •OH
(5) O2 •- / h+ / •OH / 1O2 + TC → products + CO2 + H2O

To investigate the degradation kinetics of TC in the CQDs/g-C3N4 photocatalytic reaction, LC-MS analysis and DFT calculations were performed. Fig. S6 showed the results of the successful identification of seventeen products. In addition, the TOC removal rate was measured to assess the mineralization potential of TC. As shown in Fig. S7, the TOC removal rate was about 44.25% after 150 min under visible irradiation, indicating that TC and its intermediate products might be decomposed to CO2 and H2O by RSs during the photocatalytic process.

The main reaction sites of attacked by RSs were qualitatively predicted by theoretical models of the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO), lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) and Mulliken charge distribution of TC. According to the frontier orbital theory, RSs might attack locations with high electron densities in HOMO and LUMO. In particular, O2 •- typically attacked locations with higher point charges. The results show that C7, C10, C12, C16, O24, and C26 with high electron densities were more likely to be attacked by RSs ( Fig. 9A-D). Based on the above results, possible photodegradation pathways (I and II) of TC were proposed.

As depicted in Fig. 9E, TC dissociated into protonated tetracycline (TC-H+, m/z 445) in aqueous solution[56]. In pathway I, TC molecules were attacked by h+ to generate intermediates with m/z values of 461, 445, 349, 305, 292, and 164. To begin with, reactive oxygen species (ROS) attacked the TC molecule, causing the transformation of -C9 =C11- on the ring into hydroxyl groups, resulting in intermediates with m/z 461[57]. Afterwards, •OH attacked the C26 and O24 sites to produce a product with m/z = 444 by deamidation and esterification reactions. After that, cleavage, ring opening, oxidative breakage of -COO- and -C16 =C17- occur to form the product with m/z = 349, which was further oxidized to give the product with m/z = 305[58]. The product with m/z = 444 could also be converted to m/z 292 and m/z 164 by dehydration and ring-opening reactions. In addition, ROS attacked on the product (m/z = 461) of -C16 =C17- yielding the product with m/z = 477. And then ROS attacked the C15 site to oxidize the N-methyl into carbonyl with concomitant benzene ring opening and oxidation, which led to the formation of the product (m/z = 497). In pathway II, O2 •- attacked the C7 site and the hydroxyl and methyl groups are split to produce the fragment (m/z = 414). The active species then attacked the N29, C10, C12, and C16 sites to produce products (m/z = 337) through N demethylation, ring opening, and dihydroxylation reactions[59]. Subsequently, further ring opening and hydroxylation resulted in the generation of fragments with m/z of 256, 226, 196, 166, 150, 135, and 118. Finally, some of the above intermediates were mineralized into CO2 and H2O.

The toxicity of the intermediates produced during the photodegradation of contaminants was a key factor that determined whether the photocatalysts could be widely applied. Therefore, the acute toxicity (Daphnia magna LC50), bioaccumulation factor, developmental toxicity, and mutagenicity of TC and its degradation intermediates were predicted using quantitative structure-activity relationships (QSAR) in the toxicity estimation software (T.E.S.T.) ( Fig. 10A-D). The acute toxicity of TC to Daphnia was 12.7 mg/L (categorized as "harmful "), and all intermediates except products b and f showed higher LC50 than TC, indicating reduced toxicity of the products, especially product j (LC50 of 21436.52 mg/L) (Fig. 10 A). Most intermediates showed a decrease in bioaccumulation factor (Fig. 10B), except those of c, h, and n could not be predicted, with product d decreasing by 87.4%. In addition, compared with TC, the developmental toxicity of almost all intermediates was reduced except products a and l. It was a satisfaction that, intermediates f, i, k, m, n, o, p and q became "developmental non-toxic" (Fig. 10 C). Similarly, except for products a, d, f, and g, all intermediates changed from "mutagenicity positive" to " mutagenicity negative". Especially, the mutagenicity values of products e, i, j, k, l, o, p, and q decreased significantly (Fig. 10D). Based on the above predictions, the toxicity of almost all intermediates decreased. Hence, an extended degradation time was required to achieve complete mineralization and oxidation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic degradation under simulated natural circumstances
ce:section.ce:para: The degradation of TC in various aqueous matrices was performed to determine whether the CQDs/g-C3N4 composites were suitable for natural conditions. As shown in Fig. 11 A, the removal rates of TC in tap water, lake water, and Bohai seawater decreased by 1.24%, 4.22%, and 20.24%, respectively, compared to that in deionized water (90.54%). This inhibition might be attributed to the high concentration of water-based components (like dissolved organic matter, various ions, metal complexes, and so on) that acted as filters and electron traps competing for RSs[60]. Nevertheless, the results still indicated that the CQDs/g-C3N4 system could be used to effectively degrade PPCPs under practical conditions.

The photodegradation performance under natural sunlight irradiation was evaluated to further analyze the suitability of the CQDs/g-C3N4 (Fig. 11B). Surprisingly, the degradation rate of TC reached about 93.15% within 10 min, indicating that natural light could be served as a satisfying energy source of CQDs/g-C3N4. It was noteworthy that high photocatalytic performance could always be achieved under different experimental conditions ( Table 1). In conclusion, the excellent photocatalytic achievement of the metal-free CQDs/g-C3N4 system made it of great potential value in practical wastewater treatment.

CQDs were successfully prepared for the first time through a facile hydrothermal method using waste pine branches as the carbon source, and then metal-free CQDs/g-C3N4 photocatalysts were synthesized with a thermal polycondensation process. In comparison to pure g-C3N4, the composites doping with CQDs exhibited significantly enhanced photocatalytic activities for degradation of TC under visible light irradiation. Among them, the photodegradation rate of H6 was 12.56 times greater than that of g-C3N4. The results revealed that CQDs with excellent up-conversion PL properties enhanced optical absorption and promoted the generation of additional photogenerated carriers. Meanwhile, the CQDs optimized the energy band structure, which effectively facilitated the separation of electron-hole pairs and the excellent charge transfer property. In addition, the electron acceptor function of CQDs promoted O2 reduction, resulting in significantly improving photocatalytic efficiency of the CQDs/g-C3N4. RSs trapping experiments and ESR techniques showed that O2 •- was the primary active species for TC degradation. The intermediates during the photodegradation of TC were identified by LC-MS, and the mechanism of photocatalytic of TC was investigated in depth with DFT calculations. In addition, the CQD/g-C3N4 composites had good reusability and could effectively remove PPCPs in the natural water matrix. More importantly, according to the toxicity analysis, the acute toxicity LC50 of almost all intermediates decreased significantly and the bioaccumulation factor declined. The developmental toxicity decreased to "developmental non-toxic", and the mutagenicity changed from "mutagenicity positive" to "mutagenicity negative". It was indicated that the CQDs/g-C3N4 composites were environmentally friendly and safe. Hence, this study not only offered a valuable pathway for the design of novel sunlight-driven metal-free photocatalysts but also provided a new direction for the treatment of PPCPs effluent in an environmentally compatible and cost-effective manner.

Fei Huang designed the synthesis method of CQD/g-C3N4 and proposed the idea of preparing CQD from biomass and modifying g-C3N4. Fei Huang and Dongyu Tan synthesized CQD/g-C3N4 and carried out the experimental plan. Danqin Li, Shenjia Guo and Yi Yan helped to analyze the experimental data. Fei Huang wrote the article and Wu Zhang revised the article. Copyright belongs to Fei Huang, Dongyu Tan, Danqin Li, Shenjia Guo, Yi Yan, and Wu Zhang. All members agreed to submit the manuscript to the journal Environmental Pollution.
